kept.text:
Few days ago, shared her Airbnb stories here about the mess guests leave behind.

But her biggest troubles are not even over.

Latest condition- wifi dismantled, knives damage in sofa, extreme filth, ruined linen, fan blades turned, 2 days property frozen due to all this.

Customers not picking calls, making report to airbnb takes time & efforts.

Indians are always ready to end a great service. You just rent a place, how do you get so much courage to do all this? I mean how you can even put shoes on bed, as simple as that! Let alone other damage.

Reflects a lot on your upbringing and life choices. Subhuman treatment to others makes you subhuman, not the other way round. And no! No cleaning fees or fines can be justified for it. It is a question of dignity.

kept.text:
A 16-year-old makes $12,000 a month on Airbnb without owning a single property and without leaving his room.

He rents apartments from landlords at standard long-term rates and lists them on Airbnb at short-term prices that are significantly higher. The difference — his profit from each apartment every month.

Claude analyzes the market in 20 minutes — ADR, average occupancy, net profit after all expenses and a verdict: STRONG, MARGINAL or SKIP. Nashville delivers $997 net profit from one apartment at $1,400 monthly rent and $3,219 in Airbnb revenue.

Before this it took 4-6 hours of analysis per unit — most people quit before signing their first lease. Now Claude does it in one prompt.

12 apartments across Nashville and Miami — $12,000 a month recurring. No mortgage, no personal capital — just signed rental agreements and the right listing on the platform.

A 22-year-old with 30+ properties closes $187,000 a month with the same method.

kept.text:
A woman booked an Airbnb for a stay with her family, and at first, everything felt completely normal. But not long after, she started feeling like something wasn't right. She began hearing strange noises coming from upstairs.

Curious but uneasy, she took her phone and went to check. That's when things got even stranger-balloons were moving on their own, and footsteps could be heard on the stairs... even though no one was there.

Then suddenly, something invisible tossed her dog's leash into the air.

Trying to figure out what was going on, she walked up to a closed room. As she got closer, she noticed the doorknob slowly turning from the inside, along with faint sounds coming from within.

Hoping to capture something, she slid her phone under the door to record inside the room... but the camera showed nothing....

kept.text:
Here’s my backstory on how we took Stan Store to $30m ARR:

When my co-founder and I started Stan, it was just the immigrant grind for years with lots of frugality and sheer hustle.

There was even a time when we returned an IKEA table after using it in an Airbnb for some time, asking ourselves:

"Are we building something that looks like a startup?

Or something that actually helps people make a living?"

And that question drove everything that followed.

Early on in our journey to $30M ARR, we made a decision most advisors disagreed with.

- No transaction fees to use Stan
- Flat subscription, no upsells
- $29/month price.

People told us we were leaving millions on the table by not charging a transaction fee. Like every single one of our competitors did.

And guess what? They were right.

We left the money there on purpose.

Because we realized that the moment you take a cut, you’re no longer on the Creator’s side.

You’re just the middleman.

We wanted Creators to win more because they used Stan. Not win less because we skimmed the top.

Trust compounds faster than revenue.

That turned out to be true.

As growth picked up, so did the pressure.

“Hire faster”, 
“Build a big team”, 
“Spend to show momentum.”

We ignored all of this.

Instead, we asked one question over and over:

"What breaks if we don’t hire?"

Most of the time, the answer wasn’t “people”

… but systems.

So we instead, we automated and simplified as much as we could. Deleting features. Building self-serve tools. 

Then, and only then, we hired people.

Today, Stan serves 80,000+ Creators:

They make $200M+ per year using the platform.

Three years into building, we celebrated:

~$30M ARR.
~30 people.
~7 engineers.

And a relentless focus on:

– trust
– speed
– product
– and not doing work that doesn’t need to exist

So when I see posts that talk about Stan as the “fastest-growing startup I’ve seen”…

I get it.

But the real story that needs to be told is not the one about speed.

It’s the one about restraint.

We said no more than we said yes and we didn’t optimize for looking successful. What we optimized for was being helpful and creating fundamental value for our customers.

We like to say that we didn't build Stan alone.

The Creators built it with us.

One at a time.

Over the years.

And that, out of any ARR, MRR or GMV…

That’s the only leaderboard that actually matters.

kept.text:
Facebook VP of Growth Alex Schultz on the single most important metric for startups:

Alex has advised multiple startups throughout his career, including Airbnb and Coursera. Across all of them, one pattern keeps repeating itself.

"Retention is the single most important thing for growth."

He explains how to measure it:

"If you look at this curve, percent monthly active versus number of days from acquisition, if you end up with a retention curve that asymptotes to a line parallel to the x-axis, you have a viable business and you have product market fit for some subset of market."

In other words: plot your monthly active users against the number of days since they signed up.

If that curve eventually flattens out into a horizontal line, you've found product-market fit with at least one segment of users.

If it doesn't flatten? Alex is blunt about what to do next:

"Don't go and do growth tactics. Don't go and do virality. Don't hire a growth hacker. Focus on getting product market fit. If you don't have a great product, there's no point executing well on growing it because it won't grow."

This is the trap Alex sees founders fall into again and again:

"Number one problem I've seen inside Facebook for new products, number one problem I've seen for a startups I've advised has been they don't actually have product market fit when they think they do."

The lesson?

Before you spend a dollar on growth, look at your retention curve. If it's still trending toward zero, no amount of clever marketing will save the business.

Fix the product first, then pour fuel on the fire.

kept.text:
Airbnb’s are a giant source of taxes for Mexico

Here is a typical booking for one of our properties we own in Mexico. 

It is true it’s easy to get your property on Airbnb.   That’s because the government taxes the hell out of it to the renter and owner. 

You can see what the rental guest pays and what  the owner actually gets.  
 The rest goes to the Mexican government on each stay. 

Plus the owners have to file a monthly income tax and yearly income tax filing.
